data.title: Engineers charged with stealing Google secrets, sending data to Iran
data.description:
Three former engineers were charged Thursday with stealing trade secrets from Google and other tech companies and sending the information to Iran.

Two sisters, 41-year-old Samaneh Ghandali and 32-year-old Soroor Ghandali, and 40-year-old Mohammadjavad Khosravi were indicted by a federal grand jury, according to the U.S. attorney for the Northern District of California.

While working at Google, the Ghandali sisters allegedly transferred hundreds of files, including company trade secrets related to processor security and cryptography, to a third-party communications platform.

This information was copied to their personal devices, as well as Khosravi’s work device at a separate company and Soroor Ghandali’s work device at a third company, where she and her sister later worked, according to a press release.

After Google flagged her activity and restricted her access in August 2023, Samaneh Ghandali signed an affidavit saying she had not shared confidential information outside the company.

She and Khosravi then allegedly began researching how to delete communications and data, in addition to manually photographing computer screens with trade secrets from Google and Khosravi’s employer.

The pair traveled to Iran in December 2023, where they accessed the photographs and other confidential information from Khosravi’s employer, according to the U.S. attorney’s office.

“The alleged actions outlined in this indictment reflect a calculated betrayal of trust by individuals accused of stealing trade secrets from the very tech companies that employed them,” FBI Special Agent in Charge Sanjay Virmani said in a statement Thursday.

“According to the allegations, the method in which confidential data was transferred by the defendants involved deliberate steps to evade detection and conceal their identities,” he continued. “Protecting Silicon Valley innovation and defending the groundbreaking technologies that drive our economy and national security is a top priority for the FBI.”

This is the second time in recent weeks that former Google employees have been charged with stealing the company’s trade secrets. In late January, an ex-Google engineer was convicted of stealing AI-related trade secrets from the tech giant for Chinese companies.

data.title: 58 percent say Trump administration has gone to far with undocumented immigrant deportations: Survey
data.description:
Over half of Americans in a new poll disapprove of President Trump’s sweeping crackdown on illegal immigration. 

The joint survey from The Washington Post, ABC News and Ipsos found that 58 percent of respondents believe the Trump administration has gone too far in their efforts to deport undocumented immigrants. 

This total includes 95 percent of Democrats responding to the poll and 63 percent of independent voters, but just 16 percent of Republicans. 

Additionally, a total of 62 percent of respondents said they oppose Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents’ use of force in these operations. This follows the killings of two U.S. citizens in Minneapolis in January.

An ICE agent shot and killed 37-year-old Renee Good while a Customs and Border Protection agent shot and killed Alex Pretti during federal immigration enforcement operations in the city at the end of January. The two killings intensified scrutiny of the administration’s actions, prompting calls from Democratic lawmakers for reform at the Department of Homeland Security. 

The results from this new survey are in line with trends in other recent polling data. An NBC News poll from the beginning of the month found that 60 percent of Americans disapproved, either “strongly” or “somewhat,” of how this White House has handled the issues of border security and immigration. 

The Post-ABC News-Ipsos poll was conducted between Feb. 12-17 among 2,589 U.S. adults with a margin of error of approximately two percentage points.

data.title: 5 takeaways as Supreme Court strikes blow to Trump's tariffs
data.description:
President Trump suffered a striking blow to his bid to refashion global trade with sweeping tariffs as the Supreme Court on Friday struck down his use of an emergency statute to execute the strategy core to his economic agenda. 

Trump was the first president in history to attempt to use the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to impose tariffs and had warned that an adverse ruling to his efforts would wreak havoc on the U.S. economy.  

“This was an important case to me,” Trump said during a White House press briefing, where he also made rare remarks lambasting conservative justices, including two he nominated, who ruled his use of emergency powers was unlawful.

The justices ruled 6-3 against Trump, with Chief Justice John Roberts and Justices Neil Gorsuch and Amy Coney Barrett, all conservatives, and the high court’s liberal flank joining up to cast aside the bulk of the tariffs. 

Here are five takeaways from the momentous Supreme Court decision.  

Trump spurns justices after major loss

Since returning to the White House, Trump has racked up back-to-back wins at the Supreme Court, enabling him the power to conduct mass firings, ban transgender troops, end temporary protections for Venezuelans and freeze teacher grants.

But all those were emergency rulings. 

The tariffs decision marks the first time the court has issued a final ruling on a cornerstone of one of Trump’s major second-term policies.

It’s a massive legal loss for the president, who has repeatedly described the case as one of the most important in the history of the country. The decision left the president publicly enraged. 

“The Supreme Court’s ruling on tariffs is deeply disappointing, and I’m ashamed of certain members of the court — absolutely ashamed — for not having the courage to do what’s right for our country,” Trump said at the impromptu White House briefing shortly after the opinion was issued.

Trump slammed the court’s liberal justices as “an automatic ‘No’” in cases involving his agenda and suggested they are a “disgrace to our nation.” He also asserted they were “swayed by foreign interests.” 

But the president had high praise for the three conservative justices who sided with him, none more so than Justice Brett Kavanaugh, whom he nominated to the high court in 2018 and who wrote the chief dissenting opinion. 

He said Kavanaugh’s “stock has gone so up” and said he was “proud” of his appointment. 

“I would like to thank Justice Kavanaugh for his, frankly, his genius and his great ability,” Trump said. 

Volatile day on Wall Street

The decision capped months of suspense on Wall Street, in which the stock market saw weeks of upheaval on the heels of Trump’s initial tariff announcement in April. In that aftermath, Trump regularly announced new tariff threats and trade deals with various countries and delayed implementing the duties for months, leaving markets guessing.

Friday became a volatile day of trading. Ahead of the ruling, stocks began lower in the morning following a soft GDP report. But after the decision landed, major indices rose. 

By mid-afternoon, the S&P 500 gained 0.6 percent for the day. The tech-heavy NASDAQ rose nearly 0.8 percent, while the blue-chip Dow gained about four-tenths of a percentage point. 

Last year, stocks entered bear market territory following Trump’s “Liberation Day” announcement that spurred a widespread selloff. Major U.S. indices have recovered in the months since and notched record highs in recent weeks. 

But Friday’s decision didn’t spur a market surge. They’re still waiting to see how the White House’s promise to implement new tariffs plays out. 

Some companies with significant tariff exposure, like Barbie-maker Mattel and footwear designers Deckers Outdoor Corp. and Crocs saw more significant gains Friday morning, though some pared back some of those gains as the day progressed. 

Trump’s tariffs Plan B in motion

The Supreme Court’s ruling means Trump can’t use IEEPA to justify his sweeping tariffs. But the administration has long been looking at backup options. 

They have several.  

“The good news is that there are methods, practices, statutes and authorities as recognized by the entire court in this terrible decision” Trump said Friday, claiming his new tariffs would be “even stronger.” 

Trump has already invoked Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962 to announce steel and aluminum tariffs. But Section 232 can only be used to combat imports that threaten to impair national security.

He signaled Friday that he intends to invoke Sections 122 and 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 as well.  

Trump could impose tariffs up to 15 percent for up to 150 days over unbalanced trade under Section 122 authority, but it has not been used before.  

Under Section 301 authority, he could also direct the Office of the U.S. Trade Representative to impose tariffs as an enforcement measure of Americans’ rights under trade agreements and in response to certain foreign trade practices. But that authority requires the trade representative’s office to investigate first. 

Other options include section 201 of the 1974 Trade Act and Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930.  

Kavanaugh noted these alternatives in his dissenting opinion but warned that the path forward may not be an easy one.  

“In essence, the Court today concludes that the President checked the wrong statutory box by relying on IEEPA rather than another statute to impose these tariffs,” the Trump-appointed justice wrote. “In the meantime, however, the interim effects of the Court’s decision could be substantial.” 

Gorsuch offers defense of legislative system

Gorsuch, the first Supreme Court justice Trump appointed when he first took office, joined the principal opinion in full but, in a separate concurring opinion, urged Americans to put their faith back into the legislative system. 

It was a message that seemed directed toward one person in particular: Trump. 

The conservative justice acknowledged that the court’s decision would be “disappointing” for some. He said major decisions affecting Americans are “funneled through the legislative process for a reason.” 

“Yes, legislating can be hard and take time. And, yes, it can be tempting to bypass Congress when some pressing problem arises,” Gorsuch wrote. “But the deliberative nature of the legislative process was the whole point of its design.”  

“Through that process, the Nation can tap the combined wisdom of the people’s elected representatives, not just that of one faction or man,” he continued. 

Since returning to the White House, Trump has sought to circumvent the legislative process and consolidate the executive branch’s power across the board.  

“In all, the legislative process helps ensure each of us has a stake in the laws that govern us and in the Nation’s future,” Gorsuch said. “For some today, the weight of those virtues is apparent. For others, it may not seem so obvious.  

“But if history is any guide, the tables will turn and the day will come when those disappointed by today’s result will appreciate the legislative process for the bulwark of liberty it is,” he added. 

Asked by reporters Friday if he would work with Congress, in which both chambers have  Republican majority, Trump said: “I don’t have to.” 

“I have the right to do tariffs, and I’ve always had the right to do tariffs,” the president said. “It has all been approved by Congress, so there’s no reason to do it.” 

Refund questions swirl

A major question that remains is whether importers will obtain refunds for the billions of dollars the billions in tariff revenue already collected — and how.  

For months, the Trump administration has insisted in court it won’t object to refunds. Hundreds of companies have already lined up. 

The Supreme Court gave no guidance on refunds, which Barrett previously predicted would be a “mess.” Kavanaugh referenced her remark in his dissent, expressing concern about the court’s decision not to weigh in on the path forward.  

Trump had his own complaints Friday, too.  

“They take months and months to write an opinion, and they don’t even discuss that point,” the president said, noting that his administration had taken in “hundreds of billions of dollars.” 

“Wouldn’t you think they would put one sentence in there saying that? ‘Keep the money or don’t keep the money,’” he said. “I guess it has to get litigated for the next two years.” 

Dan Anthony, executive director of We Pay the Tariffs, a coalition of small businesses opposed to Trump’s tariffs, said after the decision that the court found the money was “taken illegally” so it’s “imperative” the funds are returned without any “onerous processes.” 

“For us, it’s really about getting that money back,” he said. “Days and weeks is what a process could allow, not months and years.”

data.title: Cantwell asks Bessent for 'detailed explanation' of tariff refund process
data.description:
Sen. Maria Cantwell (D-Wash.) penned a letter to Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent asking for a “detailed explanation” of how the Department of the Treasury will dole out tariff refunds in the wake of a Supreme Court ruling Friday that said the president was working outside of his authority in imposing some tariffs. 

“Given this Administration has illegally collected hundreds of billions of dollars from American businesses, that now must be refunded, I am requesting detailed information about how th Administration plans to fairly and expeditiously reimburse the payors of those tariffs,” Cantwell wrote in her letter. 

Cantwell, the chair of the Senate Committee on Commerce, Science and Transportation, criticized President Trump’s administration for not having prepared for the Supreme Court’s decision which determined Trump could not use the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to impose tariffs on other countries.  

While the Supreme Court ruled Trump’s IEEPA tariffs unlawful, the present has already announced several other avenues he plans to use to continue his tariff regime, including an immediate 10 percent global tariff he plans to impose through Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974. 

In her letter, Cantwell argued that the financial strain caused by Trump’s tariffs has pushed some American businesses to the point of bankruptcy and demanded that the Treasury Department implement “an expeditious and transparent process to remediate the financial harm that resulted from these illegal tariffs.”

The Monthly Treasury Statement, which summarizes the financial activities of the government, includes a section called “customs duties” that includes revenue collected by the government from tariffs imposed on imported goods.

Cantwell requested that the department provide more specific data regarding the revenue collected from IEEPA tariffs including “which firms, particularly small businesses, are shouldering the frontline cash burden of these tariff taxes.” 

She asked the department to prioritize issuing refunds to small and medium sized businesses without the resources to wait for reimbursement and requested removing any additional barriers for companies to access refunds.

“All businesses must have substantial clarity that the Administration will move forward with a timely, transparent, and equitable process for refunding money to which the government is not legally entitled,” Cantwell wrote. 

She concluded her letter by asking for a response from Bessent by March 6.

data.title: Trump tariff revenue of $175B at stake after Supreme Court ruling: Estimate
data.description:
Over $175 billion in U.S. tariff revenue could be refunded after the U.S. Supreme Court ruled against President Trump’s sweeping emergency tariffs on Friday, according to economists with Penn Wharton Budget Model (PWBM), a fiscal research group at the University of Pennsylvania.

The economists’ findings, which were first reported in Reuters, note that the court’s order does not explicitly order immediate refunds. The ruling opens the possibility for refund claims to be filed, with a 180-day window after goods are “liquidated” for importers to protest and request refunds from U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP), the economists wrote.

PWBM director Kent Smetters told Reuters that most companies will seek out refunds that will “come out from the U.S. treasury.”

The estimate relies on tariff rates by product and country, including those imposed through Trump’s attempted invocation of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). CBP data estimated that the agency collected around $133.5 billion in tariffs under the IEEPA authority as of December 2025.

Trump became the first president to attempt to invoke IEEPA to impose tariffs in its nearly 50-year history. The law allows the president to “regulate” imports when necessary to respond to national emergencies that pose an “unusual and extraordinary” threat.

Justices did not address refunds in their 6-3 ruling. Justice Brett Kavanaugh was the sole justice to recognize the incoming fight over refunds, which will likely be resolved in the lower court.

“One issue will be refunds,” Kavanaugh wrote in his dissent with Justices Clarence Thomas and Samuel Alito. “Refunds of billions of dollars would have significant consequences for the U.S. Treasury. The Court says nothing today about whether, and if so how, the Government should go about returning the billions of dollars that it has collected from importers.”

Trump previously floated the idea of sending collected tariff revenue to Americans as rebate payments, telling companies and small businesses last year that they would receive refunds.

Companies like Costco, Barnes & Noble, Bath & Body Works and Yeti, among several others, lined up as the justices deliberated their decision. Hundreds of companies that have sued for their right to claim a refund are expected to see their lawsuits in trade court resume after Friday’s decision.

Trump blasted the six justices, including Chief Justice John Roberts and first-term appointees Neil Gorsuch and Amy Coney Barrett, as “very unpatriotic and disloyal to our Constitution.” He praised Kavanaugh, who he also appointed, along with Thomas and Alito “for their strength and wisdom and love of our country.”

The president said he would raise preexisting tariffs and start to impose new tariffs, as well as open investigations that could lead to import taxes on a wide range of countries and goods. Investigations sanctioned by Section 301 of the 1974 Trade Act allows the Office of the U.S. Trade Representative to use tariffs against countries deemed to have committed “discriminatory” or “unfair” trade practices.

U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer told The New York Times last month that Trump could also enact tariffs to address “large and serious United States balance-of-payment deficits” in accordance with the 1974 act. Trump said Friday that he would impose 10 percent global tariffs, as allowed by this statute, “over and above our normal tariffs already being charged.”

data.title: Vance: Supreme Court tariff decision represents 'lawlessness from the court'
data.description:
Vice President Vance condemned the Supreme Court’s decision to strike down a majority of President Trump’s sweeping tariffs on Friday.

“Today, the Supreme Court decided that Congress, despite giving the president the ability to ‘regulate imports’, didn’t actually mean it,” Vance posted on the social platform X. “This is lawlessness from the Court, plain and simple. And its only effect will be to make it harder for the president to protect American industries and supply chain resiliency.”

The vice president pointed to a “wide range of other tariff powers” still available to the president. 

Vance’s statement echoed Trump’s remarks at the White House on Friday afternoon, when he said he was “ashamed of certain members of the court.” 

A majority ruled against the Trump administration’s decision to use the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to impose steep tariffs on countries across the globe. The emergency measure grants the U.S. president the ability to place regulations on imports in response to “unusual and extraordinary” threats. 

Two of the six justices in the majority opinion — Amy Coney Barrett and Neil Gorsuch — were appointed by Trump. 

The president criticized them in his Friday remarks to the press. 

“I don’t want to say whether I regret nominating them. I think their decision was terrible,” Trump said. “I think it’s an embarrassment to their families.”

Lawmakers on both sides of the aisle have expressed discontent with the Trump administration’s use of IEEPA to impose tariffs, pointing to Congress’ constitutional authority over federal taxation. A resolution calling for the cessation of Trump’s tariffs passed in the Senate last fall with bipartisan support. 

Recent polling indicated that most Americans felt similarly, with 67 percent of respondents expressing their support for the Supreme Court to overturn these policies in a February survey. 

A recent report from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York indicated that U.S. businesses and consumers took on about 90 percent of the costs of these tariffs, despite claims from the White House that foreign countries would shoulder the majority of the financial burden. 

Several Republican lawmakers have come out supporting the court’s decision Friday. Kentucky’s GOP Sens. Rand Paul and Mitch McConnell — who both voted in favor of passing the Senate resolution last October — commended the ruling. 

Paul, who sponsored the resolution, called the ruling a “defense of our Republic” in a post on social platform X.

McConnell said in a statement that the court’s decision leaves “no room for doubt” on the issue of Congress’s constitutional authority over tariffs. 

“Congress’ role in trade policy, as I have warned repeatedly, is not an inconvenience to avoid,” McConnell said. “If the executive would like to enact trade policies that impact American producers and consumers, its path forward is crystal clear: convince their representatives under Article 1.”

data.title: Trump announces $10M in aid to East Palestine tied to 2023 train derailment
data.description:
President Trump announced late Thursday that the federal government will provide $10 million to East Palestine, Ohio, to support ongoing recovery efforts from a 2023 train derailment that triggered an environmental disaster and lingering health concerns in the community.

Trump, who has repeatedly accused former President Biden’s administration of mishandling its response to the incident, said the Economic Development Administration would allocate the money.

“To the Great People of East Palestine, Ohio, who were disgracefully betrayed and forgotten about by Crooked Joe Biden — ‘TRUMP’ has never forgotten about you, and never will!” the president wrote in a Truth Social post.

“We want to see East Palestine grow, flourish, and be able to take advantage of our now BOOMING Economy. It was horrible what was allowed to happen there, and we are with you for the LONG HAUL. MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN!” he added.

The Trump administration launched a research initiative last year to examine the health outcomes stemming from the Feb. 3, 2023, derailment, which leaked toxic chemicals and forced more than 2,000 residents to evacuate the small town located near the Ohio-Pennsylvania border.

The five-year National Institutes of Health project is aimed at studying the short- and long-term impacts of exposure to the materials carried on 38 railcars, including vinyl chloride, butyl acrylate, ethylene glycol and benzene residue — all of which are known carcinogens or pose acute irritation and respiratory risks.

Since 2023, residents living in East Palestine and surrounding communities have reported experiencing adverse health effects, including migraines, nosebleeds and memory issues.

Hundreds of residents filed a wrongful death lawsuit against Norfolk Southern in February 2024, alleging seven people had died as a “direct and proximate result of the incident and the negligence, recklessness, and other wrongful acts” of the railroad company.

Multiple other lawsuits were also brought against Norfolk Southern, leading to multi-million dollar settlements.

The derailment triggered strong political pressure on the Biden administration, with many Republicans criticizing the pace of the response.

Trump accused Biden of “indifference and betrayal” toward the community during a February 2023 campaign visit, where he was joined by his future Vice President Vance, who was an Ohio senator at the time.

Trump went on the attack again a year later as Biden prepared to travel to East Palestine to mark the first anniversary of the derailment.

“Biden should have gone there a long time ago — for him to go now is an insult to those who live and work in East Palestine, and the Great State of Ohio, itself. I can’t believe anyone wants him there?” Trump wrote on Truth Social at the time.

data.title: What are Sections 122 and 301, Trump's potential alternate tariff tools
data.description:
The Supreme Court has struck down Trump’s authority to impose tariffs on other countries through the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). But it’s not the only tool at the president’s disposal to press on with his tariff regime. 

“The reality is the president is going to have tariffs as part of his trade policy going forward,” U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said in an interview with The New York Times on Jan. 15 in response to rumblings that the Supreme Court may overthrow some or all of Trump’s tariffs. 

In the interview, Greer said if the high court did not rule in the administration’s favor, the Office of the U.S. Trade Representatives (USTR) has equipped the president with a number of mechanisms buried within the 1974 Trade Act that would allow him to continue to see through his trade goals. 

Section 301 is one statute available to Trump to implement tariffs and one that Greer listed as an option. Section 301 empowers the USTR to employ tariffs against countries they deem as having “discriminatory” or “unfair” trade practices. The president used this tool during his first term to impose tariffs on Chinese exports and the legislation survived several attempts at legal pushback at the time. 

However, limitations within Section 301 mean that Trump cannot use the statute to replace his existing tariffs right away. First, an investigation must find that a foreign country restricted U.S. commerce through discriminatory practices, which could take months.

Trump said at a press conference following the court’s ruling Friday that he was “initiating several Section 301, and other investigations, to protect our country from unfair trading practices of other countries and companies.”

Greer also mentioned Section 122 of the 1974 act, which allows the president to enact tariffs to address “large and serious United States balance-of-payment deficits.” The statute has not yet been enacted to enforce tariffs but Trump said during Friday’s press conference that he would be imposing a 10 percent global tariff using the statute “over and above our normal tariffs already being charged.” 

Prior to the ruling, Trump was using the IEEPA to implement tariffs without restrictions on duration or amount. But any Section 122 tariffs he imposes may only last for up to 150 days and are limited to 15 percent of a product’s estimated value. Congress would need to approve an extension on Trump’s threatened Section 122 tariffs to go more than 150 days. 

According to Greer, Trump could also invoke tariffs through Section 232, a statute allowing the president to impose tariffs on countries that threaten national security, or Section 338, which could allow for up to 50 percent tariffs on countries the president deems disadvantageous or discriminatory to U.S. commerce. 

The president referenced Section 232 during his comments Friday.

“Effective immediately, all national security tariffs under Section 232, and existing Section 301 tariffs — they’re existing, they’re there — remain in place, fully in place, and in full force and effect,” Trump said.

data.title: Massie seeks to undo Trump's pro-glyphosate executive order
data.description:
At least one Republican lawmaker is formally pushing back on President Trump’s effort to boost a controversial herbicide, glyphosate, that’s reviled by supporters of the Make America Healthy Again (MAHA) movement.

Trump this week stoked MAHA fury by issuing an executive order that seeks to “ensure an adequate supply” of glyphosate as a national security issue and grant “immunity” to makers of the pesticide under the Defense Production Act.

In response, Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) drafted legislation that seeks to prevent the order’s implementation. Massie’s legislation also seeks to explicitly allow citizens to bring lawsuits related to exposure to glyphosate, the key ingredient in the frequently litigated Roundup weedkiller.

“This week I will introduce the ‘No Immunity for Glyphosate Act’ to undo the recent Executive Order which promotes glyphosate (Round-Up) and insulates manufacturers from liability,” Massie, who has broken with Trump on multiple issues, wrote on X.

Rep. Chellie Pingree (D-Maine) wrote on X that she was co-leading the effort.

“The Trump Admin. cannot keep paying lip service to #MAHA while propping up Big Chemical like this and choosing corporate profits over Americans’ health,” Pingree wrote. “ENOUGH.”

While the legislation is unlikely to become law, it represents the latest in a growing fissure within the GOP, as some Republicans offer support for pesticides while MAHA-aligned individuals fight against them.

The MAHA movement, spearheaded by Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., is skeptical of big industries, including Big Pharma, Big Chemical and Big Agriculture. 

This has put the typically business-friendly Republicans at a crossroad, as some in the GOP try to push pro-pesticide language in legislation like the farm bill.

It remains to be seen if Massie, a fiscal hawk who often breaks with the party and has sharply criticized Trump’s handling of the Epstein files, will be a pro-MAHA outlier, or whether a significant number of Republican members will seek to push back against pesticides.

Kennedy had defended the president’s order in an earlier statement.

“Donald Trump’s Executive Order puts America first where it matters most — our defense readiness and our food supply,” he said. “We must safeguard America’s national security first, because all of our priorities depend on it. When hostile actors control critical inputs, they weaken our security. By expanding domestic production, we close that gap and protect American families.”

data.title: Johnson denies request for Jesse Jackson to lie in honor in Capitol
data.description:
Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) denied a request to have the late Rev. Jesse Jackson lie in honor in the Capitol, a source confirmed to The Hill.

After the civil rights leader died this week at age 84, Jackon’s family had requested that he lie in honor in the Capitol, another source said. CNN was first to report Johnson’s decision.

The Speaker considered past precedent mostly reserving the practice for former presidents and select former government officials and military honorees. Requests for conservative activist and Turning Point USA founder Charlie Kirk and former Vice President Dick Cheney to lie in the Capitol were also recently denied.

The last person to lie in state in the Capitol rotunda was former President Jimmy Carter, who died last year at age 100.

But other cultural figures have lain in honor in the Capitol rotunda in the past. Rev. Billy Graham lied in honor in the Capitol rotunda in 2018 and civil rights leader Rosa Parks lied in honor in 2005.

Jackson, a two-time presidential candidate, was remembered by the political world for his legacy as a world-renowned civil rights activist and Democratic strategist. The Rainbow PUSH Coalition, which he founded, revealed in November that he was admitted to a hospital “under observation” for PSP, a rare disorder that he had been managing for more than 10 years.  

President Trump labeled him a “force of nature like few others before him.”

He is expected to be memorialized in Chicago in the coming weeks, according to his family.

Mike Lillis contributed.
